questions.stem: A lattice in $\mathbb{R}^N$ is defined as which kind of subset?
questions.explanation: A lattice is a discrete additive subgroup of $\mathbb{R}^N$. Discreteness means every point is isolated, ruling out dense subgroups such as $\mathbb{Q}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: By Proposition 6.2, every lattice in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has which form?
options: Always $\mathbb{Z} w_1 + \mathbb{Z} w_2$ with $w_1, w_2$ linearly independent.
$\{0\}$, or $\mathbb{Z} w_1$, or $\mathbb{Z} w_1 + \mathbb{Z} w_2$ with $w_1, w_2$ linearly independent.
questions.explanation: A lattice in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is one of three types: the trivial lattice $\{0\}$, a rank-1 lattice $\mathbb{Z} w_1$, or a rank-2 lattice $\mathbb{Z} w_1 + \mathbb{Z} w_2$ spanned by two independent vectors.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A $2$-dimensional Euclidean crystallographic group is defined as which kind of subgroup?
options: Any subgroup of the translation group.
questions.explanation: A crystallographic group is a discrete subgroup of $\mathrm{Isom}(E^2)$. Its translations form a lattice, and its image under $\varphi$ in $O(2)$ is the point group.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The point group of a crystallographic group $G$ is which object?
options: The set of fixed points of $G$.
The image $\varphi(G)$ of $G$ in $O(2)$ under the orthogonal-part homomorphism.
questions.explanation: Applying $\varphi : \mathrm{Isom}(E^2) \to O(2)$ to $G$ gives the point group, a subgroup of $O(2)$. The kernel of $\varphi$ restricted to $G$ is the lattice of translations.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The crystallographic restriction (Lemma 7.2) states that a rotation in the point group of a crystallographic group must have which orders?
questions.explanation: The crystallographic restriction allows only rotations of order $2$, $3$, $4$, or $6$. Order $5$ and orders above $6$ are impossible because they would force a lattice vector shorter than the shortest one.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which rotation order is explicitly forbidden by the crystallographic restriction?
questions.explanation: Order $5$ is forbidden. If a point group had an order-$5$ rotation $R$, then $w_1 + R^2 w_1$ would be a lattice vector closer to the origin than the shortest one, a contradiction.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many wallpaper groups (rank-2 Euclidean crystallographic groups) are there?
questions.explanation: There are exactly $17$ wallpaper groups, the symmetry groups of patterns that are periodic in two independent directions.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A frieze pattern has a symmetry group that is a crystallographic group of which rank?
questions.explanation: Frieze patterns are those whose symmetry group is a rank-1 crystallographic group: the translations form a lattice $\mathbb{Z} w$ in a single direction.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rank of a crystallographic group $G$ is defined as which quantity?
options: The rank of its lattice of translations.
The order of its point group.
The number of reflections it contains.
questions.explanation: The rank of a crystallographic group is the rank of its translation lattice $\Lambda$: $0$ for finite groups, $1$ for frieze patterns, $2$ for wallpaper patterns.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A rank-0 crystallographic group (lattice $\{0\}$) must be which kind of group?
options: A finite cyclic or dihedral group.
A group containing a glide reflection.
questions.explanation: With trivial lattice the group has no translations or glide reflections, so it consists of the identity, rotations, and reflections about a common point. Hence it is finite, cyclic or dihedral by Proposition 3.2.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: By Lemma 7.1, each element of the point group of a crystallographic group acts on the lattice $\Lambda$ in which way?
options: It maps $\Lambda$ to a proper subset.
It sends every lattice vector to $0$.
questions.explanation: The point group maps the lattice onto itself: if $A : x \mapsto Bx + v$ is in $G$ and $w \in \Lambda$, then conjugating the translation by $w$ shows $Bw \in \Lambda$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A rank-2 lattice that admits an order-4 rotation in its point group must be which lattice?
questions.explanation: If the smallest rotation has angle $\pi/2$ (order $4$), then $w_1$ and $w_2 = R w_1$ are perpendicular and equal in length, generating the square lattice.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A rank-2 lattice that admits an order-3 or order-6 rotation in its point group must be which lattice?
options: A rectangular lattice with unequal sides.
questions.explanation: When the smallest rotation has angle $2\pi/3$ or $\pi/3$, the vectors $w_1$ and $w_2 = R w_1$ generate the hexagonal lattice, which supports both order-3 and order-6 symmetry.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: By Corollary 7.3, the point group of a $2$-dimensional crystallographic group is one of which families?
options: Cyclic groups $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4, C_6$ or dihedral groups.
questions.explanation: The point group is cyclic $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4, C_6$ or one of the corresponding dihedral groups. The crystallographic restriction forbids $C_5$ and any cyclic group of order greater than $6$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The square of a glide reflection is always which kind of isometry?
questions.explanation: Squaring a glide reflection gives a translation (by twice the glide vector). This is why a rank-0 group, having no translations, can contain no glide reflections.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many frieze groups (distinct symmetry types of rank-1 patterns) are there?
questions.explanation: There are $7$ frieze groups, the distinct symmetry types of patterns periodic in one direction. They are built from translations together with possible half-turns, reflections, and glide reflections.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: For a frieze group with translation $T : x \mapsto x + w$, the point group is a subgroup of a group of order $4$ generated by which maps?
options: The identity, a half-turn $R$, and reflections $M$, $N$ in the lines along and across $w$.
An order-5 rotation and a reflection.
questions.explanation: An orthogonal map fixing the rank-1 lattice $\mathbb{Z} w$ must be the identity, the half-turn $R$, the reflection $M$ in the line along $w$, or the reflection $N$ across it. These four maps form a group of order $4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A lattice $\Lambda = \mathbb{Z} w_1 + \mathbb{Z} w_2$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has a fundamental set given by which region?
options: The parallelogram $\{t_1 w_1 + t_2 w_2 : 0 \le t_1, t_2 < 1\}$.
questions.explanation: The half-open parallelogram spanned by $w_1$ and $w_2$ contains exactly one point of each coset, so it is a fundamental set. Gluing its opposite sides gives the torus $\mathbb{R}^2/\Lambda$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A crystallographic group whose point group contains an order-6 rotation forces the rotation to act on a lattice. Which smaller rotation order is automatically also present in the point group?
questions.explanation: An order-6 rotation $R$ has $R^2$ of order $3$ and $R^3$ of order $2$, both in the point group. So order-6 symmetry on a lattice forces orders $3$ and $2$ as well, consistent with the hexagonal lattice.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The quotient $\mathbb{R}^2/\Lambda$ of the plane by a rank-2 lattice $\Lambda$ is which surface?
questions.explanation: Identifying the opposite sides of the fundamental parallelogram for a rank-2 lattice produces a torus, so $\mathbb{R}^2/\Lambda$ is a torus.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which of these orders of rotation is allowed in a wallpaper group but, together with its forbidden neighbour, illustrates the crystallographic restriction?
options: Order $4$ is allowed while order $5$ is forbidden.
Order $5$ is allowed while order $4$ is forbidden.
Order $7$ is allowed while order $6$ is forbidden.
questions.explanation: Order $4$ is allowed (square lattice) but order $5$ is forbidden. The jump from $4$ to $6$ skipping $5$ is the signature of the crystallographic restriction.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Why does the rotation through angle $\pi$ (order $2$) always map any rank-2 lattice onto itself?
options: Because it fixes every lattice vector.
Because it sends each lattice vector $v$ to $-v$, which is also in the lattice.
Because it is a glide reflection.
questions.explanation: The half-turn sends $v \mapsto -v$. Since a lattice is an additive group, $-v$ is always in the lattice, so every rank-2 lattice has at least order-2 rotational symmetry about the origin.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Give the number of distinct wallpaper groups (rank-2 Euclidean crystallographic groups in the plane).
questions.solution: The number of wallpaper groups is {{1}}.
questions.explanation: There are exactly $17$ wallpaper groups.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Give the number of distinct frieze groups (symmetry types of patterns periodic in one direction).
questions.solution: The number of frieze groups is {{1}}.
questions.explanation: There are $7$ frieze groups.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The crystallographic restriction permits rotation orders $2, 3, 4, 6$ in a wallpaper group. State whether an order-5 rotation is permitted.
questions.solution: Order $5$ is excluded by the crystallographic restriction, so it {{1}} permitted.
questions.explanation: An order-5 rotation would generate a lattice vector shorter than the shortest one, so it is not permitted.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Count the allowed rotation orders for a rotation in the point group of a $2$-dimensional crystallographic group (the allowed orders are $2, 3, 4, 6$). Give how many distinct orders are allowed.
questions.solution: The allowed orders are $2, 3, 4, 6$, so there are {{1}} of them.
questions.explanation: The four allowed nontrivial rotation orders are $2, 3, 4, 6$, giving $4$ distinct values.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
